figures.gloss: The Eternal Blue Sky itself — not a sky behind which a god sits but the sky that is the god: vast, impartial, the giver of the kut (fortune, mandate) by which a khan rules and a people endures. The Orkhon inscriptions open by invoking him: 'When the blue sky above and the brown earth below were made, between the two were made the sons of men.'
figures.facet: A sky-father by pure function, not descent: where Dyeus and his cognate sons are reconstructed from a single Indo-European word, Tengri is the same archetype reached down an utterly separate road — proof that the joint is a shape the human eye finds in the sky, not an heirloom passed hand to hand.
figures.provenance: Earliest firm attestation in the 8th-c. Old Turkic Orkhon inscriptions; the term spans Turkic *teŋri and Mongolic tngri across two millennia and many peoples, so 'Tengrism' names a family of related skies, not one creed.

figures.gloss: The womb-goddess — her very name an old word for placenta and womb — guardian of mothers, infants and the new-born soul, often pictured as a woman with sixty golden tresses descending from the sky to ward the cradle. To Tengri's high impartial sky she is the near, warm power that tends the threshold of birth.
figures.facet: The mother imagined as the organ of bearing itself: not earth-as-field nor sea-as-deep but the placenta personified — the great-mother joint touched at its most intimate biological root, fertility named for the flesh that feeds the unborn.
figures.provenance: Attested among Old Turkic peoples via the Orkhon inscriptions' invocation-triad (Tengri, Umai, Yer-Sub) and recorded in depth by 19th–20th-c. ethnography among the Altai, Khakas, and Kirghiz (Radloff, Potapov); the name is cognate with Mongolic umai/umay, 'placenta, womb'.

figures.gloss: Lord of the lower world and the dead, the dark first-made who dived for the earth and kept a fragment back, and so seeded sickness, death and the night-side of creation; he rules the nine-layered underworld and sends his messengers up for the souls of men.
figures.facet: The same figure is judge of the dead AND the principled spoiler of creation — underworld-sovereign and adversary fused in one body, so that death and evil are not separated here as they are where a neutral Hades faces a separate devil. His name carries to the Tibetan-Buddhist Yama (Erlik/Yama), a junction where two underworlds met.
figures.provenance: Best recorded among the Altai Turks and Siberian peoples by 19th–20th-c. ethnographers (Radloff, Anokhin); the Erlik–Ülgen creation-rivalry shows clear later contact with Buddhist and dualist (Manichaean/Zoroastrian) framings, to be weighed when reading it as 'pure' Tengrism.

figures.gloss: The bright maker of the upper world among the Altai, who shaped the earth, the first man and the useful things, and to whom the white shaman ascends through the sky-layers in the great horse-sacrifice; the benign creator-half to Erlik's dark half.
figures.facet: A creator who must be climbed to, layer by sky-layer, rather than prayed down: Ülgen's worship is an ascent, the shaman riding the offered horse-soul up through the heavens — godhead reached by journey, the smith-maker met at the top of a vertical road.
figures.provenance: Best recorded among the Altai Turks in 19th–20th-c. ethnography (W. Radloff, A.V. Anokhin); the Ülgen–Erlik creation-rivalry shows clear later contact with Buddhist and dualist (Manichaean/Zoroastrian) framings, per this roster's own Erlik entry.

figures.gloss: The Siberian shaman — the type-specimen from whom the very word 'shaman' is taken (Evenki/Tungus šaman) — who in trance and drum climbs the world-tree or pillar through upper, middle and lower worlds, escorting souls, retrieving the strayed and the stolen, bargaining with Erlik for the sick.
figures.facet: Here the axis-mundi is not a static cosmic landmark but a road actually travelled in the body: the tree, the notched pole, the smoke-hole are rungs a living person ascends and descends in ecstasy — the world-pillar defined by the act of climbing it, cosmology as technique.
figures.provenance: 'Shaman' enters the world's languages through 17th-c. Russian reports of Tungus practice; Eliade's classic synthesis universalised the Siberian type but also flattened local variety — read the 'three worlds' as a widespread Siberian pattern, not a single fixed dogma.

figures.gloss: The great tree or iron pillar at the navel of the earth whose roots drink the lower world and whose crown brushes Tengri's sky, about which the heavens turn on the Pole Star — the nail that pins the cosmos, the ladder the shaman's soul ascends, mirrored on earth in the tent-pole and the sacred birch.
figures.facet: The axis figured as the still nail of a turning sky: the North Star is the peg through the world-pole's top, and the whole heaven a tethered horse circling it — a cosmology built from what the steppe nomad actually watched wheel above him each night.
figures.provenance: A widespread Siberian and Inner Asian motif recorded across Altai, Yakut (Sakha, e.g. the Aal Luuk Mas), and Buryat ethnography (Radloff, Harva/Holmberg); the layered-sky detail of the cosmology shows some later influence from Buddhist Mount Meru cosmology in areas of Buddhist contact.

figures.gloss: On the first all-covering water, a being dives to the bottom for a fragment of mud, from which the dry earth is spread and grows — in the Altai and wider Siberian telling, a contest between the bright maker and a dark diver who hides a pinch of soil and so makes the world wider and his own dominion of death within it.
figures.facet: The diver here is doubled into rivals, so the creation-dive becomes the origin of evil itself: the earth grows large precisely because the dark one held some back — the same cosmogonic gesture the Haudenosaunee tell as the muskrat's selfless death is told here as the first deceit.
figures.provenance: The earth-diver motif forms a genuine circum-Arctic belt linking Siberia and Native North America — a real distributional link of great age, but one of shared inheritance or deep diffusion, NOT documented historical contact between the specific traditions.

figures.gloss: The vital fortune-force granted from the sky: the kut that makes a khan legitimate, a hunt prosper, a child live — and also the soul itself, which the shaman must guard, for a lost or stolen kut is sickness and a fled kut is death. To rule is to hold the sky's kut; to fall is to have it withdrawn.
figures.facet: Sovereignty as a substance the sky lends and can recall: the Turkic mandate is not a covenant or a law-code but a fortune-fluid issuing from Tengri — political order and personal life-force named by the very same word, so that losing the throne and losing the soul are one kind of loss.
figures.provenance: Attested directly in the 8th-c. Orkhon inscriptions as the sky-granted fortune legitimating the qaghan; comparative Turkic linguistics (G. Clauson, An Etymological Dictionary of Pre-Thirteenth-Century Turkish) documents the same word carrying both 'soul' and 'fortune' senses across Turkic dialects, including Yakut kut-sür.

figures.gloss: The holy Earth-and-Water of the homeland — the collective sacred powers of soil and river invoked in the 8th-century Orkhon inscriptions alongside Tengri and Umai as protectors of the Türk realm. Not one spirit but the sanctified land itself, addressed as 'ıduk Yer-Sub'.
figures.facet: Where Tengri is the impartial far sky, Yer-Sub is the sky's earthly counterpart in the oldest attested stratum — sovereignty here rests on a triad (sky, earth-water, cradle-mother), not a single high god plus a passive earth.
figures.provenance: Attested directly in the 8th-c. Old Turkic Orkhon (Kül Tigin, Bilge Qaghan) inscriptions, alongside the Tengri and Umai invocations.

figures.gloss: The sacred mountain-forest of the Orkhon valley named in the inscriptions as the seat from which the qaghan must rule; to hold Ötüken was to hold heaven's mandate, and the inscriptions themselves warn the Türk people never to abandon it for other lands.
figures.facet: The mandate is territorial before it is personal: kut is granted from the sky, but it is anchored to one physical holy ground, so that losing Ötüken and losing the right to rule are described as the same catastrophe.
figures.provenance: Named repeatedly in the 8th-c. Orkhon inscriptions (Kül Tigin and Bilge Qaghan monuments) as the qaghanate's holy seat.

figures.gloss: The remote supreme creator of recorded Altai Turkic cosmogony, set above or identified with Ülgen, from whom Ülgen and the other high powers are said to proceed; the apex the working sky is climbed toward but who himself does little.
figures.facet: A high god behind the high god: Kayra Khan shows the withdrawn-creator pattern doubling back on itself — even Ülgen, already remote atop the sky-layers, needs a still-more-recessed source to have proceeded from.
figures.provenance: Recorded in 19th-c. Altai ethnography (V. Verbitsky, W. Radloff); an Altai-specific figure, not attested in the older Orkhon-inscription stratum — read as one regional articulation of the apex-creator, not a pan-Turkic dogma.

figures.gloss: The she-wolf of the Türk royal origin-myth, recorded in the Chinese dynastic histories (Zhoushu, Suishu): she rescued the sole surviving boy of a destroyed clan, bore his sons in a mountain cave, and from that union the Ashina — the Göktürk ruling line — descend. Wolf-headed standards carried the claim before the qaghan's armies.
figures.facet: Kut is granted from the sky, but the Ashina also claim it in the blood: Asena roots the qaghanate's legitimacy in totemic descent, a first-ancestor register that runs beneath and beside the sky-mandate theology.
figures.provenance: Recorded in the Chinese dynastic histories Zhoushu and Suishu (compiled 7th c.) as the origin legend of the Ashina clan of the Göktürk qaghanate.

figures.gloss: Felt, wood, or cloth effigies housing ancestral or helper spirits, kept in the yurt, fed with offerings, and consulted in household ritual — the material heart of everyday Turkic-Mongolic domestic religion.
figures.facet: Against Tengri's vast impersonal sky, the ongon is the sacred made small and handleable: a spirit bound into an object the family can feed, move, and address directly, the tangible end of a cosmology otherwise pitched at the scale of heaven.
figures.provenance: Documented ethnographically across Turkic and Mongolic peoples of Siberia and Inner Asia (Radloff, Harva/Holmberg); the household ongon-cult is a distinct, widely attested register from the state-level sky cult.

figures.gloss: The best-documented Altai ceremony: over two or three nights a horse is consecrated and its soul escorted by the white shaman up through the sky-layers to Ülgen, the ascent narrated aloud step by step as the shaman climbs a notched birch pole set up for the rite.
figures.facet: Cosmology performed rather than merely told: the tailga makes the shaman's sky-layers and Ülgen's remoteness into a literal, danced, all-night itinerary with a named destination and a return.
figures.provenance: Recorded in detail by W. Radloff and A.V. Anokhin among the Altai Turks (19th–early 20th c.); already referenced within this roster's own Ülgen entry.

figures.gloss: Stone cairns raised at mountain passes and peaks to honor the local land-masters and the sky; travelers circle them three times sunwise, adding a stone or leaving an offering before continuing their journey.
figures.facet: Where Ötüken is the one holy center a whole qaghanate must hold, the ovoo multiplies that logic into countless small, local axes — every pass gets its own miniature holy mountain, tended not by the state but by whoever passes through.
figures.provenance: The most visible living Tengrist/steppe practice, documented across Mongolia, Buryatia, and Inner Asia into the present; underlies this roster's revival ('living: true') claim.

figures.gloss: The mother-spirit of the hearth fire, honored with the first portion of food and drink and guarded by taboos against putting anything unclean into the flames — the resident sacred presence of the yurt itself.
figures.facet: Umai guards the cradle from the sky's threshold; Ot Ana is the same female, near, protective register brought all the way down into the household fire — the domestic sacred at its most literally warm and present.
figures.provenance: Hearth-fire veneration is documented across Turkic and Mongolic peoples as a distinct domestic cult, complementing the Umai birth-cult in the near, intimate register of the tradition.

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Alevi Dede's hereditary authority represents a documented syncretism, layering Islamic Alid lineage over the Turkic concept of sacred, charismatic bloodlines.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Semah's ecstatic flight represents a historical retention of Central Asian shamanic trance practices, syncretized into Sufi mystical rotation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An overarching sky-father who forms the absolute canopy of the cosmos but rarely micromanages human affairs.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Subterranean sovereigns commanding the cold realms where human souls eventually descend and dissolve.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The primal figure who introduces mortality and disease into a previously unblemished creation.

The lha/nyen/klu sky-rock-water tiers rhyme with the Siberian shaman's upper/middle/lower worlds — both an indigenous Inner-Asian three-world cosmos; structural kinship across the plateau and the steppe, no proven descent.

The three-tier spirit-world implies a vertical axis joining sky, surface and depths, as the Tengrist world-pillar does; the Bön axis is realised separately in the swastika-mountain and the mu-cord. Structural parallel of the three-storeyed cosmos.

Great-mother across Inner Asia, but inverted in altitude: Umai is the near womb that warms the cradle, Satrig Ersang the metaphysical wisdom-womb enthroned at the summit — same joint, opposite register.

The gshen/bönpo as ecstatic technician who heals, ransoms souls and binds spirits parallels the Siberian shaman's office; the Bön divergence is the braiding of that craft with narrative legitimation (drung) and a secret riddle-language (deu).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct linguistic and conceptual descent from the ancient Turco-Mongolic supreme sky deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fierce, immanent goddess of the physical earth and womb who balances the remote masculine sky.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Direct descent from the ancient Siberian and Turco-Mongolic lord of the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The exact shared Siberian cosmological framework of navigating vertical realms to negotiate with spirit lords.
GOLD SEAM: The vital, transactional favor of heaven that legitimizes authority, but which can be withdrawn if the mandate is not properly exercised.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The distant, overarching sky-deity or principle of the upper world who oversees existence but rarely interferes.
GOLD SEAM: The vast, overarching Heaven that confers the political mandate to rule based on the sovereign's virtue and alignment with cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme, non-anthropomorphic sky creator who is virtually indistinguishable from the physical atmosphere and vast heavens.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent a manipulable, quasi-physical substance of divine majesty that legitimizes earthly rule.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme, over-arching sky who bestows the mandate of kingship upon earthly rulers.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The bóveda functions structurally as an axis mundi, establishing a vertical column of light through which elevated spirits can descend to the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The overarching, somewhat removed sky-god who embodies the physical heavens.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The lord of the underworld and creator of pests in neighboring Siberian and Mongolic cosmologies.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The classic ecstatic mediator who bridges upper, middle, and lower realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central pillar of the universe connecting the celestial heights with the subterranean depths.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The spiritual life-force that simultaneously acts as the sacred mandate for political sovereignty and earthly authority.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the vast, remote, but ultimately benevolent blue sky whose intervention is sought primarily in dire crises.
Mana beside Turkic-Mongol kut — both a transmissible sacred fortune/power that gods bestow and that legitimates the ruler; the holy charge of chiefship and luck, reached in two unconnected worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The grim, subterranean lord who commands the spirits of illness and strictly enforces the reality of human mortality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmological framework of a shaman utilizing spirit helpers to navigate vertically between upper, middle, and lower realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme, blue-sky creator of the Eurasian expanse.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Siberian lord of the underworld, situated conceptually 'downward' and serving as the structural counterpart to the sky creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fierce, immanent Siberian earth mother who safeguards childbirth and the physical span of life.

Loose: both are dark lords of death and the underworld set against a bright sky-god, but Erlik sits within a layered shaman cosmos while //Gaunab is a battlefield adversary who wounds the god directly. Shared darkness, different machinery. Flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The transcendent, blue-sky creator who is intimately linked to high mountain peaks.
Two North/East-Asian high heavens that legitimate kingship and recede from the lived world — the sky-father reached down a non-Indo-European road, convergent not inherited. Never a cognate.

Both the shamanic crossing between worlds, but inverted in direction: the Siberian shaman ASCENDS the world-tree in ecstasy, while the Korean mudang is most often DESCENDED-INTO and possessed — flight versus indwelling as the mode of the sacred road. Northeast-Asian shamanic kinship is real but the relation is structural, not documented descent.

Both the near, archaic North-Asian mother-power; Ungnyeo diverges as a totemic she-bear who earns humanity, where Umai is the womb personified — two roots of the great-mother in one cultural belt, parallel not proven-shared.
Both the household guardian of birth and infancy petitioned by mothers and watching the cradle — closely parallel North-Asian birth-mother spirits, structural kinship without proven contact.
Both the holy height where heaven meets earth, the road of the descent; sansin diverges by distributing the axis across every local peak rather than one cosmic tree-pillar.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A vital, heaven-mandated spiritual energy inherited by rulers that confers absolute political authority and physical vitality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A vast, overarching sky deity whose divine will is synonymous with the physical atmosphere and vast horizons.

The ecstatic three-world soul-traveller reached on the far side of the Andes: the machi's küymin trance, drum and ascent treat with the upper world and retrieve the strayed soul exactly as the Siberian type — convergent shamanic technique, normatively in a woman's body here.

The step-notched rewe is a near type-match to the Siberian shaman's notched world-tree/sky-pillar — the axis figured as a ladder of layers the shaman climbs in the body; long noted, read as convergence not contact.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A quantifiable life-force or divine favor that grants earthly success and can be empirically lost.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The specific Mongolian articulation of the ancient, pan-Turco-Mongol cult of the Eternal Blue Sky.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both fulfill the role of the nurturing maternal terrestrial force in the steppe nomadic cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Mongolian adoption and continuation of the ancient pan-Turkic lord of the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The vast, overarching blue sky deity of the Eurasian steppe and tundra.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Siberian lord of the underworld who demands sacrifices and stands in constant opposition to the creator god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The exact shared Siberian cosmological framework of navigating vertical realms to negotiate with spirit lords.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The fierce, immanent goddess of the physical earth and womb who balances the remote masculine sky.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Harner explicitly extracted the Siberian and Central Asian three-world cosmology to formulate his standardized, universally accessible map for the journey.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The palais center-post serves the exact function of the shamanic pillar: the vertical axis down which spirits descend to mount their human horses.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Spiritual Baptist visionary 'travels' map onto a tiered cosmology where the seeker traverses distinct metaphysical realms to progress.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The supreme sky father of animist cosmologies who is geographically distant and must be specifically invoked for vital weather.

The Sámi noaidi and the Siberian shaman are two members of one great North-Eurasian shamanism: drum-trance, the three worlds, the soul sent out and the descent to ransom the sick — a real circumpolar pattern, shared inheritance or deep diffusion rather than documented contact.

The drum is the Sámi axis-mundi made portable — where the Siberian shaman climbs the world-tree, the noaidi reads the three worlds off the painted membrane he strikes; the same vertical cosmos held in different objects.

The near, warm mother-power of birth complementing a remote sky — Máttaráhkká's household of birth-mothers beside the Inner-Asian womb-goddess Umai; the great-mother met at the threshold of the cradle, no descent claimed.

The trance-journey to speak with the spirits — cohoba's plant-borne oracle answering the Siberian shaman's three-world flight; the same office of the soul-traveler, here delivered through snuff and the speaking idol.
The high impartial sky and the near womb-goddess form the sky/earth, far/intimate pairing — Tengri grants the kut, Umai guards the cradle that receives the new soul.
Bright upper maker and dark lower keeper, rivals in the earth-diver creation — a creator/spoiler dyad whose sharpness owes something to later Buddhist and dualist contact.
Same cosmology from two sides: the tree/pillar IS the road, the shaman's flight is the act of climbing it. (Structural identity within one tradition, not linguistic cognation.)
The shaman descends to Erlik's nine-layered underworld to bargain back the souls of the sick — psychopomp and underworld-sovereign locked in the same rite.
Kut is what Tengri grants and withdraws — the sky-god and the fortune-force are the giving and the gift.

Two East/Inner-Asian high heavens reached without Indo-European descent — impartial Sky as legitimating mandate (kut / Tianming); centuries of steppe-China contact make some mutual influence likely but the parallel itself is structural.
The sky-father shape reached down a wholly separate (Altaic, non-IE) road — the brightest proof that this joint is convergent, NOT an Indo-European inheritance. Never a cognate.
Both a single supreme sky-power who legitimates rulership and recedes from images; a speculative reach across families, flagged as such.
Great-mother to a sky-father, but Umai diverges sharply — she is the womb/placenta personified, fertility named for the bearing-flesh rather than the earth's body.
Both the warm near mother-power complementing a remote supreme; analogous function, no descent or contact claimed.
Through Tibetan-Mongolian Buddhism, Erlik is directly identified with Yama (Erlik Nomun Khan) as judge of the dead — a documented religious syncretism, not a mere parallel.
Both sovereigns of the realm of the dead; Erlik diverges by also being the cosmic spoiler — judge and adversary in one, where Hades is morally neutral.
A benign creator-maker met at the topmost sky-layer, paired against a destructive double — analogous high-creator function across families.

Odin's nine-night hanging on the world-tree to win wisdom rhymes hard with the shaman's ascent of the world-tree — and some scholars suspect real circumpolar diffusion behind the resemblance, though it stays unproven (tier 3).
Both are the conductor of souls across the boundary; the shaman diverges by making the crossing a living ecstatic journey he returns from, not a one-way ferry.
World-tree at the cosmic centre joining the three worlds — one of the strongest North-Eurasian axis-mundi convergences; circumpolar diffusion is plausible but undemonstrated.
Both the vertical axis about which the heavens revolve, mirrored in built ritual form (sky-pillar / cosmic mountain); some of the layered-cosmos detail entered Altai cosmology via Buddhism.
The Pole-Star-as-nail and the turning sky-as-tethered-horse is this shard's distinctive contribution to the axis-mundi joint — the axis defined by what the steppe sky actually does overhead.

The genuine circum-Arctic earth-diver belt: Siberia and the Haudenosaunee muskrat share one ancient cosmogonic motif. A real distributional link of great antiquity — shared deep inheritance/diffusion, not documented contact, so tiered 3 not 1.

Same earth-from-the-deep cosmogony; the Siberian telling diverges by doubling the diver into rivals so the dive births evil, where the Turtle-Island telling makes it an act of consent and self-sacrifice.

Kut as Heaven's mandate to rule rhymes pointedly with the Chinese Mandate of Heaven (Tianming) — the sky's withdrawable warrant for a sovereign; the steppe-China political contact makes this rhyme partly historical.
Both a diffuse indwelling sacred potency rather than a personal god; kut diverges by binding political legitimacy and the living soul into one substance the sky lends.
A single animating sacred force underlying many beings (one-through-many) — analogous impersonal-power concepts across wholly separate worlds.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A loose parallel of a specialized practitioner actively crossing boundaries to forge binding, reciprocal alliances with potent otherworldly intelligences.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The monotheistic, vast sky deity whose eternal, unchangeable nature is linked conceptually to the sheer expanse of the heavens above.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme Turkic/Mongolic sky fathers, historically derived from the same steppe cosmology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The benevolent white creator of the upper world in Siberian Turkic mythologies.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dark sovereign of the underworld and source of sickness in broader Siberian belief.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The central cosmic pillar linking the three realms of the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A structural parallel to the Turkic goddess of fertility, children, and the earth.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The classic ecstatic traveler who mediates between cosmic realms to retrieve stolen souls.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Yakut 'kut' is derived from the same linguistic and spiritual concept as the Tengrist 'kut' (vital spirit or heavenly blessing).

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the soul-travelling ritual specialist as the community's working core — recitation-led soul retrieval and guidance of the dead; typological kinship only, no demonstrated descent.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a sky-power paired with an earth-mother counterpart (as Tengri with Umai) — the Inner-Asian sky-frame in Himalayan dress; structural analogy, not a proven lineage.
Chuvash Turӑ is the Oghur-Turkic reflex of the same sky-name as Tengri — direct linguistic descent through Volga Bulgar; the westernmost living branch of the Tengri word.

The same office of dealing with the spirits on the community's behalf, but diverged in technique: the Siberian shaman flies the three worlds in trance while the yumӑҫ diagnoses and settles accounts by divination — the ecstatic specialist domesticated.

Diószegi argued the táltos is inherited Siberian-steppe shamanism surviving in Hungary; the typological kinship (election by mark and sickness, animal-form spirit-battle, trance) is real, but genetic descent remains contested — tiered as analogy, not descent.

The tale-tree the marked boy climbs beside the notched pole the shaman ascends — Diószegi read them as one inheritance, but the seam is held at structural kinship: the same vertical cosmos, the Hungarian one surviving only as story.

Heaven's withdrawable warrant to rule, delivered in steppe idiom — the Turul-dream legitimates the Árpáds as the sky's kut legitimates a khan; the Magyars were a steppe polity, so influence is plausible, but no document ties the two, hence analogy.

The near mother-power guarding childbed and infant — Boldogasszony's birth-bed customs beside Umai's cradle-watch; a structural kinship of office, with the Hungarian figure herself a reconstruction read out of Marian syncretism.
Typological seam only: two supreme, imageless, temple-less high powers over a customary-law society — no linguistic or historical descent claimed across the Caucasus/steppe line.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: a structurally identical Inner Asian dualist pairing — a benevolent sky-creator brother opposed by a dark underworld twin (Seveki/Khargi echoing Ülgen/Erlik).

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the Hungarian folk devil's dive for primal earth is a direct structural rhyme with the Tengrist earth-diver cosmogony, both drawing on the widespread Eurasian earth-diver motif.
The same diving-bird-fetches-mud cosmogony documented across the wider Uralic-Siberian culture area; a structural and likely diffusional parallel, not claimed direct descent.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: the requested explicit seam with the tengrist shard — the same ecstatic-specialist office described for the wider Turco-Mongol steppe-religion complex that mongol-folk's own cosmology (99 tngri, ongon) is a regional articulation of.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both are Inner Asian personal/dynastic vital-force concepts tied to fortune and rule, though distinct terms — sülde is the banner-housed guardian-genius, kut is heaven-granted fortune — within the same Turco-Mongol charisma-of-rulership complex.
The oldest attested Türk state cult invokes them together: the sky above and the sacred earth-water below, the Orkhon inscriptions' own opening pairing.
Ötüken is the holy center within the wider sacred Earth-Water — the specific seat inside the general sanctified homeland, both named in the same inscriptions.
To hold Ötüken is, in the inscriptions' own logic, to hold heaven's mandate — the sacred ground and the fortune-force that legitimates rule are bound together.
Altai cosmogony sets Kayra Khan as the remote apex from whom Ülgen proceeds — a doubled withdrawn-creator, one recessed behind the other; Altai-specific, later-recorded material.
Two coexisting warrants for Ashina rule: kut granted downward from the sky, and wolf-descent claimed upward through the blood — sovereignty argued two ways in the same qaghanate.

Totemic beast-ancestress myths grounding a people's or ruling line's descent — the Türk she-wolf beside the Korean she-bear; structural kinship of the founding-animal-mother pattern, not shared descent.
Between journeys, the shaman's helper-spirits are often kept housed in ongon effigies — the ecstatic office and the household fetish-vessel are working partners in the same practice.
The tailga is Ülgen's own cult act — the concrete rite behind the deity entry's own reference to 'the great horse-sacrifice'.
The tailga is the shaman's three-world ascent given a specific, dated, communally witnessed liturgical form.
The single cosmic axis of the world-tree/sky-pillar multiplied into countless local shrines at every pass and peak — the same vertical-holy logic redistributed across the landscape.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: stone-cairn/rock cult at outdoor sacred sites, circled and given offerings by travelers — a widespread Eurasian pattern of the local numen made of stone, not shared descent.

Loose: both add stones to a cairn while passing a sacred spot in reverence/petition, but the Khoekhoe rite is tied specifically to Heitsi-Eibib's grave-cairns, a different mythic anchor. Shared gesture, different machinery.
Two near, warm, female sacred presences of the intimate domestic sphere — Umai at the cradle, Ot Ana at the hearth that warms it.

Both name a heaven-allotted personal fortune central to a Turkic-descended people's fate-doctrine — Pülekh as a personified distributing deity, Tengrist kut as an impersonal apportioned life-force; areal/genetic Turkic kinship of idiom, not identical offices.
both are Turkic/Siberian goddesses of childbirth and the child's soul, within the same wider Aiyy/Tengrist religious complex.

Mongolian Buddhism ran the identification the other direction from Erlik's regional descendants — Yama himself was rendered as Erlik Nomun Khan, fusing the shamanic spoiler-of-creation with the dharma-king of the dead under the Turkic name.

dossier.essence: The indigenous sky-centered religion of the Turkic and Mongolic peoples of the Central Asian steppe and Siberia, focused on Tengri, the 'Eternal Blue Sky,' attested among the Xiongnu and named explicitly in the 8th-century Old Turkic Orkhon inscriptions. It is less a single creed than a family of related sky-and-ancestor cults spanning two millennia and many peoples; since the 1990s it has been consciously revived as a national 'Tengrism' in parts of Central Asia and Mongolia. Its central claim is that the sky confers fortune and the mandate to rule (kut), mediated between a tiered cosmos by shamans.
dossier.cosmos: The universe is tiered — an upper sky-world, the middle earth, and a lower underworld — joined by a world-tree or pillar about which the heavens turn on the Pole Star. Tengri presides above; the womb-mother Umai guards birth, and Erlik rules the dead below. Fortune, legitimacy, and the soul itself are all 'kut,' lent by the sky and recallable, so that losing the throne and losing one's life are named by one word; at death the soul may pass to the underworld, escorted or retrieved by the shaman.
dossier.practice: In the historic period there was no scripture or standing priesthood: cult centered on the ruler's veneration of the sky, sacrifices of horses and libations of mare's milk, and the honoring of ancestors and sacred mountains (Otuken). The specialist is the shaman (Turkic kam, Mongolic boo), who drums into ecstatic trance to travel the three worlds, heal, and divine. An ethic of oath, hospitality, and cosmic order (toru) governs conduct, and modern revivals have added codified festivals and rites.
dossier.canon: No native scripture; the record is the 8th-century Orkhon and Yenisei runiform inscriptions (Kul Tegin, Bilge Khagan, Tonyukuk), Chinese and Islamic chronicles, the Mongol 'Secret History' (13th century), and 19th-20th-century ethnography of surviving Altai and Siberian practice (Radloff, Anokhin).
dossier.reflects: The sky as sovereign — the reading of political legitimacy and personal fate alike as a single fortune lent from heaven and withdrawable at its will.
sources: Jean-Paul Roux, 'La religion des Turcs et des Mongols' (1984)
Uno Harva, 'Die religiosen Vorstellungen der altaischen Volker' (1938)
Marlene Laruelle, 'Religious Revival, Nationalism and the Invention of Tradition: Political Tengrism in Central Asia and Tatarstan' (2007)
